items.myth: Lower training loss always means better test performance.
items.reality: Overfitting can drive training loss down while validation loss rises. Loss measures fit on seen data; generalization depends on model capacity, data quality, and regularization.
items.example: A 10-layer net hits train CE ≈ 0.01 but val CE rises after epoch 5 — memorizing labels, not generalizing.

items.myth: MSE and RMSE are different losses you must choose between for training.
items.reality: RMSE = √MSE. Minimizing MSE minimizes RMSE (monotonic transform). RMSE is usually an evaluation metric; MSE is the typical training objective because it is smooth and sums cleanly.
items.example: Same predictions: MSE=100 implies RMSE=10. Gradient descent on MSE finds the same ŷ as minimizing RMSE.

items.myth: Cross-entropy and BCE are unrelated to regression losses.
items.reality: All are supervised losses scoring ŷ vs y. BCE and CE come from H(P, Q) (see Cross-entropy topic); MSE/MAE score continuous error magnitude. The unifying idea is empirical risk minimization.
items.example: One pipeline uses MSE for price prediction; another uses CE for category — both minimize (1/n) Σ L(ŷᵢ, yᵢ).

items.myth: MAE is always better than MSE when outliers exist.
items.reality: MAE is more robust, but MSE may still be preferred when large errors are genuinely costlier or when Gaussian MLE assumptions hold. The choice is problem-dependent.
items.example: Sensor calibration with rare glitches: MAE helps. Predicting financial risk where tail errors are catastrophic: MSE or Huber may be intentional.
